Hi this is my elevated pitch. Can you please give me feedback if this research topic is good.

**Research Question**:
"How do brief mindfulness practices influence classroom climate in Reception children?"

**What**:
This study will investigate the impact of brief mindfulness practices (1-5 minute interventions) on classroom climate in Reception-aged children, focusing on peer interactions, engagement, and teacher-student relationships.

**Why It’s Original**:
While mindfulness has been extensively studied in older students through long-term programs, there is limited research on how brief, daily mindfulness interventions affect Reception-aged children(ages 4-5), particularly in terms of overall classroom climate and social dynamics.

**Why It’s Important**:
Classroom climate plays a critical role in early years education, affecting social development, behavior, and learning outcomes**. Understanding how mindfulness can improve these dynamics offers practical, low-cost solutions for schools looking to enhance classroom environments, reduce disruptions, and promote emotional well-being.

**How**:
Using a mixed-methods approach, this study will combine qualitative observations of classroom behavior (peer cooperation, student engagement, and teacher-student interactions) with quantitative assessments (teacher feedback and behavioral checklists) over several weeks to measure the influence of brief mindfulness practices on classroom climate.

Comment:
Don't overthink:
So if you got a couple of ideas already, try them out and see if you can use those ideas/details and connect or talk about different aspects of your life. Need to be able to find ideas or topics you have a lot to say about (in other words elastic). If you find there's not much you can write about relating to those details, probably not as elastic.
You need to try to make a simple outline out first before you will know whether or not your topic is going to work (like if you have much to say about it).

Title: How to Write Topic Sentences in Essays, IELTS, TOEFL, &. More
Description: In this English writing lesson, learn how to write topic sentences for essays, paragraph assignments, IELTS, TOEFL, and more.
Comment:
😀Hi everyone! Thanks for watching! Please don't forget to show your support and give this video some love! And let me know if you have any questions.

✍ PRACTICE:
Write topic sentences for the following writing prompts:

What are the best ways to improve your English at home? (write just 1 paragraph)

What are the reasons people should go to college? (write an essay; you will need 2-4 topic sentences)

Share in the comments!

TIPS:
1. Use the formula: The first reason/step/benefit of ... is ...
2. Include the topic, controlling idea, and key point of the paragraph.
3. If you're writing topic sentences for an essay, add variety by using synonyms and/or moving the key point to the front.

Comment:
Does the personal statement need to have a "sense of future" with an example? What I mean is that does it need to showcase an event or topic, a theme/analysis, and a "setup for how the person will carry those themes into the future."
Most personal statements I've read now have an event with a theme and some characteristics that it reveals about the writer. Usually these themes and characteristics are developed toward around the third quarter of the essay. Then, toward the end, the writer mentions an example of something that they did after the realization or reveal of the theme, which embodies those traits.
For instance, an essay I read was about a girl's grandmother, and how the grandmother's existence and later illness taught the girl to value time and devote this time to others. Then, toward the end of the essay, the author provided an example of volunteering at a nursing home, where she played music to the residents.
I noticed that the first essay had the themes and traits that he revealed in the various chunks/scenes. However, it seems like it lacked concrete example of something they did AFTER the those events to embody those traits. My draft is somewhat similar, where I talk about a topic and reveal what it says about me, but I am struggling to show how I a will carry these traits with me into the future. Is a real-world example of this "POST-analysis/realization thing" necessary?

Title: Identifying topic sentence and main idea.
Comment: I know a method to find the implied main idea! 1. Find the topic. It’s usually a repeated word or phrase. 2. Look for the common bond in the details. Ask what all these details have in common. The common bomd is the controlling idea. 3. Combine the topic and controlling idea to make the main idea. 4. Test the main idea by asking it a question. If all the details answer the questions, then the main idea is correct. Remember the supporting details explain the main idea whereas the main idea summarizes the details.

So, the problem for me with this particular matter ist the general distinction of the topic and the subject of a sentence. In most European languages the subject of a sentence is usually perceived as the topic. The subject is the "acting" part (when there is a verb describing the action) and the object is the part that's being acted on. There is no specific topic that could be deviating form the subject or the action.
It might have been helpful using "identical" Korean sentences - e.g. "The cat is cute" - in the two versions with 은/는 and 이/가 and then explain the difference in their meaning to Koreans. I was particularly puzzled by why the cheetah is the topic when it's fast, and when it's slow it suddenly becomes the subject - i.e. no longer the topic. Maybe you can elaborate on that.

Title: How to Use 은/는/이/가 ?! Korean Subject & Topic Markers
Description: 안녕하세요 여러분 ! In order to help someone who has struggled to understand 은, 는 (Korean topic markers) and 이,가 (Korean ...
Comment:
Thank you for making this video! I have watched about 3-4 vids on subject/object markers in korean but was still confused by the end of them. With this video, it was really helpful and I finished the video understanding the concepts of 이, 가, 는, 은. Thank you so so much for explaining, you've helped me get better at korean :)

My notes (ignore):

은 / 는 - topic markers
- used for General statements and facts

이, 가 - subject markers
-used for specifics

Ends with consonants- use 은, 이
Eg: 이 것 은 or 이 것 이

Ends with vowels use 는, 가
저 는 or 제 가 (I'm formal)

General fact: use 은 or 는
Eg: 사 과 논 발 개 요.
Apples are red.

Topic = 사 과 (apples)
* ends with vowel ㅘ so use 는

Eg. 2 지 타 는 빨 라 요
Cheetah are fast
Topic: cheetah


은:
E.g 오 늘 은 일 요 일 이 에 요.
General fact so use 은.
* does not end in vowel

는 and 은 is also used for contrast.
E.g I only eat seafoods - would use 는 to show i don't eat seafood only shrimp (contrast).

이 and 가 used when you observe or describe something
* always use if verb is descriptive verb
*also used for specifics

If a cheetah is moving at a zoo and I see it.
지 타 가 느 리 네 요
That cheetah is slow
* topic (지 타) ends in vowel so 가 is used
* could say 지 타 는, but that would mean cheetas being slow is a general fact and wouldn't directly relate to the specific cheetah I am watching.

Eg. 2 거 양 이 귀여 워
The cat is cute
* word ends in consonant, and it is a specific fact.

Eg.3 짐 이 많 으 시 네 요.
You have a lot/much luggage
* we are observing so we use 이.
* ends in consonant

more examples
Q: who ate it? ( being asked specific)
Since it is specific, we use either 이 or 가. 

Answer: 제 가 먹 었 어 요.
I ate it all.

Used 가 because word before it ends in a vowel.

Eg.2 이 사 람 이
This guy
* specific statement
*ends in constinat so we use 이.

versus each other
Q: what is chulsu doing right now?
Answer: 절 수 는 __ (enter activity)
* General statement
*ends in vowel (ㅜ) so we use 는.


However, if the Question was:
Q: who went to do it?
Answer: 절 수 가 갔 어 요.
* specific
*topic in vowel (ㅏ)

Contrast
* this coffee is hot
거 비 가 뜨 거 워 요.
* specific (we are not talking about all the coffees in the world)
*ends in vowel (이).

However if we take a sip of another coffee and we want to compare/contrast the temperature, we would use 는.

이 거 비 는 안 뜨 거 워 요.
This coffee isn't hot.
* we are contrasting
* ends with vowel so we use 는 instead of 은.

Hamburgers taste good
* General statement so we would use 는 after the word 햄 버 거.

However if talking about a specific hamburger,

이 것 햄 버 거 가 맛 있 어 요.
This hamburger is good
*specifics
* ends with vowel so we use 가.
